title: Algebraic Non-Integrability Argument for the Three-Body Problem

background.liouville_integrability: A Hamiltonian system with n degrees of freedom is Liouville integrable if there exist n independent, Poisson-commuting integrals of motion F_1,...,F_n (with F_1 = H). 'Independent' means dF_1 ∧ ... ∧ dF_n ≠ 0 on an open dense set. 'Poisson-commuting' means {F_i, F_j} = 0.
background.three_body_dof: The three-body problem in d dimensions has 3d position + 3d momentum = 6d phase-space dimensions, hence n = 3d degrees of freedom. Removing center of mass and total momentum reduces this to n = 3d - d = 2d independent degrees of freedom in the center-of-mass frame.
background.classical_result: Poincaré (1890) proved non-integrability of the restricted three-body problem using analytic continuation of periodic orbits. Bruns (1887) showed no algebraic integrals exist. These proofs use specific techniques and apply to restricted settings.

our_approach.construction: Start with the C(N,2) pairwise Hamiltonians H_ij. Generate a Lie algebra by computing all iterated Poisson brackets. Compute the exact dimension (rank over QQ) of this algebra at each bracket level.
our_approach.key_observation: For the 1/r potential with N=3, d=1: the algebra has dimension [3, 6, 17, 116] through level 3 and appears to grow without bound.
our_approach.degrees_of_freedom: For N=3, d=1: 3 degrees of freedom in the full system, 2 in the CM frame. For Liouville integrability, we need 2 independent commuting integrals (plus H itself = 3 total).

strong_form.claim: If the Lie algebra generated by pairwise Hamiltonians is infinite-dimensional, then no finite set of polynomial integrals constructed from these Hamiltonians can render the system Liouville integrable.
strong_form.logic: Suppose F_1,...,F_n are n commuting integrals, all expressible as polynomial combinations of elements of the Lie algebra. Then {F_i, H_total} = 0 for all i. But {F_i, F_j} = 0 means the F_i generate an ABELIAN subalgebra of dimension n. In a Lie algebra of dimension D, the maximum dimension of an abelian subalgebra is bounded. If the algebra keeps growing and its structure does not admit large abelian subalgebras, integrability is obstructed.
strong_form.gap: The argument has a gap: the Lie algebra growing does not by itself prevent the existence of n commuting elements. A solvable Lie algebra of any dimension has large abelian subalgebras (its derived series terminates). We need structural information about the algebra — specifically, the dimension of its center and commutant.

weak_form.claim: The dimension of the Lie algebra generated by pairwise Hamiltonians is a MEASURE OF COMPLEXITY of the dynamical system. The sequence [3, 6, 17, 116, ...] growing without stabilization is strong evidence against integrability.
weak_form.logic: For known integrable systems (harmonic oscillator with 1/r^-2 potential), the algebra closes at finite dimension 15. The 1/r algebra grows super-exponentially with no sign of closure. This is consistent with Poincaré's non-integrability result and provides a quantitative algebraic measure of 'how non-integrable' the system is.
weak_form.strength: This is evidence, not proof. But it's COMPUTABLE evidence that applies uniformly across all mass ratios (by the mass invariance theorem).

what_we_can_prove.statement_1: The Lie algebra generated by pairwise 1/r Hamiltonians under the Poisson bracket has dimension at least 116 at bracket level 3, for all mass ratios. (PROVEN — exact rank over QQ and Q(m1,m2,m3).)
what_we_can_prove.statement_2: The algebra is solvable of length 3 and nilpotent of class 3 at the level-2 subalgebra (rank 17). (PROVEN — derived and lower central series computed.)
what_we_can_prove.statement_3: The center of the level-2 algebra has dimension 11 (out of 17). (PROVEN.)
what_we_can_prove.statement_4: The algebra dimension is invariant under changes of mass, spatial dimension (d=1,2,3), and is universal across all 1/r^n potentials (n≥1) at levels 0-2. (PROVEN for tested cases.)
what_we_can_prove.statement_5: The harmonic oscillator (r^2) is the ONLY potential in our test suite that produces a finite algebra (dim 15, closing at level 2). All other potentials — both singular and polynomial — grow to at least 116. (PROVEN.)

what_we_cannot_yet_prove.infinite_dimension: We have not yet proven the algebra is infinite-dimensional. This requires either (a) showing level 4 adds new generators (the mpmath computation was interrupted), or (b) a theoretical argument that the algebra cannot close.
what_we_cannot_yet_prove.formal_non_integrability: A formal non-integrability proof from the algebra dimension requires either the strong form argument (which needs more structure theory) or a connection to differential Galois theory (Morales-Ramis type arguments).

path_to_proof.option_1: Complete the level 4 computation. If rank increases beyond 116, the algebra is still growing at level 4 and the pattern [3, 6, 17, 116, L4] with L4 > 116 would be strong evidence for infinite dimension. Two additional level-4 data points (different N values) could confirm the growth pattern.
path_to_proof.option_2: Connect to Morales-Ramis theory. The Morales-Ramis theorem states that if a Hamiltonian system is integrable, the differential Galois group of the variational equations along any particular solution must be abelian. The dimension of our Lie algebra is related to the structure of these variational equations. A large, non-abelian Lie algebra is exactly what Morales-Ramis obstructs.
path_to_proof.option_3: Use the solvability structure. We know the level-2 algebra is solvable. If the full algebra (including level 3+) is NOT solvable, that has direct implications for the existence of abelian subalgebras of sufficient dimension.

bottom_line: We have strong computational evidence for non-integrability but not yet a complete proof. The evidence includes: (1) algebra dimension 116 >> 3 required for integrability, (2) growth rate accelerating across levels, (3) the unique integrable case (harmonic) being the only one with a finite algebra, (4) mass invariance showing this is a generic property. Completing the level 4 computation or connecting to Morales-Ramis theory would close the gap.
